title: Quiz: Euler's Formula
description: Test your understanding of Euler's formula and its applications in quantum computing

questions.question: What is the value of e^(iœÄ)?
questions.explanation: By Euler's formula: e^(iœÄ) = cos(œÄ) + i¬Ζsin(œÄ) = -1 + i¬Ζ0 = -1. This is the famous Euler's identity: e^(iœÄ) + 1 = 0.

questions.question: What is the polar form of z = 1 + i?
questions.explanation: For z = 1 + i: |z| = βàö(1¬≤ + 1¬≤) = βàö2, and ΈΗ = arctan(1/1) = œÄ/4. So z = βàö2 ¬Ζ e^(iœÄ/4).

questions.question: When multiplying zβ²¹ = rβ²¹e^(iΈΗβ²¹) and zβ²² = rβ²²e^(iΈΗβ²²), what happens to the angles?

options.text: Angles multiply: ΈΗ = ΈΗβ²¹ Ο½ ΈΗβ²²

questions.explanation: When multiplying complex numbers in polar form: zβ²¹¬Ζzβ²² = rβ²¹rβ²²¬Ζe^(i(ΈΗβ²¹+ΈΗβ²²)). Magnitudes multiply, angles add.

questions.question: What is e^(iœÄ/2)?
questions.explanation: e^(iœÄ/2) = cos(œÄ/2) + i¬Ζsin(œÄ/2) = 0 + i¬Ζ1 = i. This corresponds to a 90¬Α rotation on the complex plane.

questions.question: The complex conjugate of e^(iΈΗ) is e^(-iΈΗ).
questions.explanation: True! (e^(iΈΗ))* = (cos ΈΗ + i sin ΈΗ)* = cos ΈΗ - i sin ΈΗ = e^(-iΈΗ). Taking the conjugate negates the angle.

questions.question: How many distinct fourth roots does the complex number 16 have?
questions.explanation: Every non-zero complex number has exactly n distinct n-th roots. So 16 has 4 fourth roots: 2, -2, 2i, and -2i (evenly spaced on a circle of radius 2).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly express cos(ΈΗ) using Euler's formula? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) True: This is the standard formula derived from Euler's formula. b) True: e^(iΈΗ) = cos(ΈΗ) + i¬Ζsin(ΈΗ), so Re(e^(iΈΗ)) = cos(ΈΗ). c) False: This formula gives sin(ΈΗ), not cos(ΈΗ). d) False: |e^(iΈΗ)| = 1 for all ΈΗ, not cos(ΈΗ).
